text_summary: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) was the most common serious adverse event in the ELIANA pivotal trial (NCT02435849) of tisagenlecleucel in pediatric/young adult r/r B-ALL. CRS occurred in 77% of patients (any grade), with 46% experiencing grade 3-4 CRS per the Penn Grading Scale. Median onset was day 3 post-infusion (range 1-22 days) with median duration of 8 days. High tumor burden (>50% marrow blasts) and higher peak CAR-T expansion correlated with severe CRS (PMID: 29385376).
incidence_rate: 77% any grade, 46% grade 3-4
management_protocol: Tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV for grade 2+ CRS; corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h) for tocilizumab-refractory cases; ICU monitoring for hemodynamic instability

text_summary: In the ZUMA-1 pivotal trial (NCT02348216) of axicabtagene ciloleucel for r/r large B-cell lymphoma, CRS occurred in 93% of patients, with 13% experiencing grade 3+ events per the Lee criteria. Median onset was day 2 (range 1-12 days) with median duration of 7 days. Peak IL-6, ferritin, and CRP levels correlated with CRS severity. Two patients (2%) required vasopressor support. CRS resolved in all patients (PMID: 28187985).
incidence_rate: 93% any grade, 13% grade 3+
management_protocol: Tocilizumab per institutional CRS management algorithm; corticosteroids for refractory CRS; siltuximab considered for tocilizumab-refractory cases

text_summary: Brexucabtagene autoleucel (Tecartus) demonstrated CRS in 91% of patients with r/r mantle cell lymphoma in the ZUMA-2 trial (NCT02601313). Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 15% of patients. Median time to onset was 2 days (range 1-13 days) and median duration was 11 days. One patient experienced grade 5 CRS. Management with early tocilizumab and bridging therapy optimization reduced severe CRS in an updated safety cohort (PMID: 32511981).
incidence_rate: 91% any grade, 15% grade 3+
management_protocol: Tocilizumab for grade 2+; dexamethasone for refractory CRS; bridging corticosteroids prior to infusion to reduce tumor burden

text_summary: Lisocabtagene maraleucel (Breyanzi) showed a favorable CRS profile in the TRANSCEND NHL 001 trial (NCT02631044) for r/r large B-cell lymphoma. CRS occurred in 42% of patients (any grade) with only 2% grade 3+ events per ASTCT consensus grading. The lower CRS rate compared to other CD19-targeted CAR-T products is attributed to the defined CD4:CD8 composition and separate manufacturing of T-cell subsets. Median onset was day 5 (range 1-14 days) (PMID: 33049860).
incidence_rate: 42% any grade, 2% grade 3+
management_protocol: Tocilizumab for persistent grade 2 or grade 3+ CRS; supportive care and antipyretics for grade 1; defined CD4:CD8 composition contributes to lower baseline CRS risk

text_summary: Idecabtagene vicleucel (Abecma) targeting BCMA for r/r multiple myeloma demonstrated CRS in 84% of patients in the KarMMa trial (NCT03361748). Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 5% of patients per ASTCT grading criteria. Median onset was day 1 (range 1-12 days) with median duration of 5 days. CRS in BCMA-targeted CAR-T tends to present earlier than CD19-targeted products. Elevated baseline LDH and high tumor burden were predictive of grade 3+ CRS (PMID: 33626253).
incidence_rate: 84% any grade, 5% grade 3+
management_protocol: Tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV for grade 2+; corticosteroids for refractory cases; close hemodynamic monitoring in first 72 hours due to early onset pattern

text_summary: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Carvykti) targeting BCMA demonstrated CRS in 95% of patients in the CARTITUDE-1 trial (NCT03548207) for heavily pretreated r/r multiple myeloma. Grade 3-4 CRS occurred in 4% of patients. Median time to onset was 7 days (range 1-12 days), notably later than other BCMA CAR-T products, attributed to the dual-epitope binding design. Median CRS duration was 4 days. All CRS events resolved with tocilizumab and/or corticosteroids (PMID: 35658469).
incidence_rate: 95% any grade, 4% grade 3-4
management_protocol: Tocilizumab per ASTCT guidelines; corticosteroids for refractory CRS; later onset allows outpatient monitoring in select patients after initial observation period

text_summary: Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) occurred in 39% of patients receiving tisagenlecleucel in the ELIANA trial, with 13% grade 3-4 events. ICANS typically presented concurrent with or following CRS, with median onset on day 6. Manifestations included encephalopathy, confusion, aphasia, and somnolence. Higher pre-infusion disease burden and peak serum IL-6 levels were associated with ICANS severity. ICE score monitoring was implemented per ASTCT consensus guidelines (PMID: 29385376).
incidence_rate: 39% any grade, 13% grade 3-4
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring q8h; dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h for grade 2+; high-dose methylprednisolone 1 g IV for grade 3+; anti-seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam

text_summary: ICANS was particularly prevalent with axicabtagene ciloleucel in ZUMA-1, occurring in 64% of patients with 28% grade 3+ events, the highest neurotoxicity rate among approved CD19 CAR-T products. Severe cases included cerebral edema in 1% of patients. ICE score decline and elevated CSF protein were early indicators. Endothelial activation with blood-brain barrier disruption, measured by elevated angiopoietin-2:angiopoietin-1 ratio, is a key pathogenic mechanism (PMID: 29440070).
incidence_rate: 64% any grade, 28% grade 3+
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring q4-8h; dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h for grade 2; high-dose methylprednisolone for grade 3+; intrathecal therapy considered for refractory cases; seizure prophylaxis mandatory

text_summary: ICANS occurred in 63% of patients treated with brexucabtagene autoleucel in ZUMA-2, with 31% grade 3+ events. Three patients (3%) developed grade 5 neurologic events. The high ICANS rate in MCL may reflect disease-specific factors including CNS involvement. Prophylactic corticosteroids during lymphodepletion and early intervention with dexamethasone were associated with reduced grade 3+ ICANS in a protocol-amended safety cohort (PMID: 32511981).
incidence_rate: 63% any grade, 31% grade 3+
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring q4h; prophylactic dexamethasone on days 0-2; aggressive early intervention with corticosteroids for grade 2; anakinra considered for steroid-refractory cases

text_summary: Lisocabtagene maraleucel demonstrated a lower ICANS rate in TRANSCEND NHL 001, with 30% any-grade and 10% grade 3+ neurotoxicity. The defined-composition product with separate CD4+ and CD8+ CAR-T cell components may contribute to reduced neurotoxicity. Median onset was day 8 post-infusion. No fatal neurotoxic events were observed. ICANS resolved in all patients within a median of 5 days (PMID: 33049860).
incidence_rate: 30% any grade, 10% grade 3+
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring q8-12h; dexamethasone for grade 2+; supportive care with anti-seizure prophylaxis; lower baseline risk allows extended outpatient monitoring

text_summary: ICANS with idecabtagene vicleucel in KarMMa occurred in 18% of patients (any grade), with 3% grade 3+ events. Neurotoxicity was generally mild and self-limited in BCMA-targeted CAR-T compared to CD19-targeted products, potentially due to lower CNS inflammatory response. Movement and neurocognitive adverse events distinct from classical ICANS were reported in 3% of patients and are under ongoing investigation (PMID: 33626253).
incidence_rate: 18% any grade, 3% grade 3+
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring; dexamethasone for grade 2+; corticosteroids for grade 3+; neurological evaluation for atypical presentations including movement disorders

text_summary: ICANS was reported in 17% of patients receiving ciltacabtagene autoleucel in CARTITUDE-1, with 2% grade 3-4 events. However, a unique delayed neurotoxicity pattern was identified in 5% of patients, presenting 3-8 weeks post-infusion with cranial nerve palsies, peripheral neuropathy, and parkinsonian-like features distinct from acute ICANS. This non-ICANS neurotoxicity led to an FDA class-wide safety investigation and label updates (PMID: 35658469).
onset_timing: Acute ICANS: median day 8; delayed neurotoxicity: 3-8 weeks post-infusion
incidence_rate: 17% any grade ICANS, 2% grade 3-4; 5% delayed neurotoxicity
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring for acute ICANS; extended neurological follow-up for 8+ weeks; MRI brain for delayed symptoms; corticosteroids and IVIG for delayed neurotoxicity

text_summary: Prolonged cytopenias (lasting >30 days post-infusion) are a major post-market safety concern for all CAR-T products. In real-world data from the CIBMTR registry for axicabtagene ciloleucel, grade 3-4 neutropenia persisted beyond day 30 in 33% of patients, thrombocytopenia in 29%, and anemia in 17%. Prolonged cytopenias were associated with prior lines of therapy (>3), severe CRS, and baseline cytopenias pre-lymphodepletion (PMID: 34407410).
onset_timing: Persisting beyond day 30 post-infusion; biphasic pattern with initial recovery then late relapse
incidence_rate: 33% prolonged neutropenia, 29% prolonged thrombocytopenia, 17% prolonged anemia (>30 days)
management_protocol: G-CSF for neutropenia (avoid in first 21 days due to potential CRS exacerbation); TPO-receptor agonists for refractory thrombocytopenia; transfusion support; bone marrow biopsy if persistent >90 days

text_summary: In long-term follow-up of the ELIANA trial, prolonged cytopenias were observed in 49% of pediatric patients at day 28. By 3 months, 24% still had grade 3-4 cytopenias. Tisagenlecleucel fludarabine/cyclophosphamide lymphodepletion contributed to myelosuppression. A biphasic cytopenia pattern was described: initial recovery by day 14-21 followed by a secondary nadir at day 28-42 correlating with peak CAR-T expansion. Growth factor support improved neutrophil recovery (PMID: 30428784).
onset_timing: Biphasic: initial nadir day 0-14, secondary nadir day 28-42; 24% persistent at 3 months
incidence_rate: 49% at day 28, 24% persistent at 3 months
management_protocol: G-CSF (filgrastim 5 mcg/kg/day) after day 21; platelet and RBC transfusion support; infection prophylaxis with antibacterials and antifungals during neutropenia

text_summary: Cytopenias are the most common adverse event with idecabtagene vicleucel, with grade 3-4 neutropenia in 89%, thrombocytopenia in 52%, and anemia in 60% during KarMMa. Prolonged cytopenias (>1 month) were particularly notable in multiple myeloma patients due to extensive prior therapy including alkylators and immunomodulatory agents. Hematopoietic reserve assessed by pre-lymphodepletion CBC and bone marrow cellularity predicted prolonged cytopenia risk (PMID: 33626253).
onset_timing: Onset during lymphodepletion; 37% with grade 3+ at day 30
incidence_rate: 89% grade 3-4 neutropenia, 52% grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia, 60% grade 3-4 anemia overall
management_protocol: G-CSF after day 14; TPO-receptor agonists (eltrombopag) for persistent thrombocytopenia; transfusion thresholds: platelets <10K, hemoglobin <7 g/dL; marrow evaluation at day 60 if unresolved

text_summary: In CARTITUDE-1, ciltacabtagene autoleucel caused grade 3-4 neutropenia in 95%, thrombocytopenia in 68%, and anemia in 44%. Prolonged hematologic toxicity (>2 months) occurred in 41% of patients. The heavily pretreated myeloma population (median 6 prior lines) had limited marrow reserve. Two deaths attributed to prolonged cytopenias with secondary infections were reported. Extended cytopenia duration correlated with number of prior lines and baseline platelet count <75,000/mcL (PMID: 35658469).
onset_timing: Onset during lymphodepletion; 41% with prolonged cytopenias >2 months
incidence_rate: 95% grade 3-4 neutropenia, 68% grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia, 44% grade 3-4 anemia
management_protocol: G-CSF from day 14; aggressive antimicrobial prophylaxis during prolonged neutropenia; transfusion support; consideration of autologous stem cell boost for persistent aplasia >90 days

text_summary: Infections are a leading cause of non-relapse mortality post-CAR-T therapy. In a multicenter real-world analysis of axicabtagene ciloleucel recipients (n=238), infections occurred in 48% of patients within the first 90 days: bacterial (29%), viral (23%), and fungal (8%). Invasive fungal infections (Aspergillus, Candida) occurred predominantly during prolonged neutropenia. Infection-related mortality was 4.6%. Hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG <400 mg/dL) was present in 43% and associated with 2.3-fold higher infection risk (PMID: 34543381).
onset_timing: Peak incidence days 0-30; ongoing risk through 12 months post-infusion
incidence_rate: 48% any infection within 90 days; 8% invasive fungal; 4.6% infection-related mortality
management_protocol: Antimicrobial prophylaxis: levofloxacin, acyclovir/valacyclovir, fluconazole/posaconazole; PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX; IVIG replacement for IgG <400 mg/dL; CMV and EBV monitoring

text_summary: In the ELIANA long-term follow-up, 43% of pediatric patients experienced infections within 8 weeks of tisagenlecleucel infusion. Serious infections (grade 3+) occurred in 24%. Respiratory viruses (RSV, influenza, parainfluenza) were the most common viral pathogens. B-cell aplasia, a pharmacodynamic effect of CD19 CAR-T, leads to persistent hypogammaglobulinemia requiring IgG replacement therapy. Vaccination with inactivated vaccines is recommended starting 6 months post-infusion, though efficacy may be reduced (PMID: 30428784).
onset_timing: 43% within 8 weeks; ongoing risk due to B-cell aplasia
incidence_rate: 43% any infection within 8 weeks, 24% grade 3+
management_protocol: Antimicrobial prophylaxis per institutional guidelines; IVIG 400 mg/kg monthly for IgG <400 mg/dL; revaccination schedule beginning 6 months post-CAR-T; avoid live vaccines indefinitely

text_summary: Infection complications with idecabtagene vicleucel are compounded by the immunosuppressed state of heavily pretreated multiple myeloma patients. In KarMMa, grade 3+ infections occurred in 22% of patients. Opportunistic infections included Pneumocystis jirovecii (1.5%), CMV reactivation (5%), and herpes zoster (8%). Hepatitis B reactivation screening is mandatory prior to lymphodepletion. Prophylactic antimicrobials are recommended for a minimum of 6 months post-infusion (PMID: 33626253).
incidence_rate: 22% grade 3+ infections; 5% CMV reactivation; 8% herpes zoster
management_protocol: Acyclovir prophylaxis for VZV/HSV; TMP-SMX for PJP prophylaxis; HBV screening and entecavir if HBsAg+; weekly CMV PCR monitoring for 3 months

text_summary: COVID-19 emerged as a significant infectious complication in post-market CAR-T safety data. A multicenter analysis of 77 CAR-T recipients (including Breyanzi patients) with SARS-CoV-2 infection showed 25% mortality, compared to 3% in the general population. Prolonged viral shedding (median 40 days) and impaired seroconversion due to B-cell aplasia complicated management. COVID-19-directed monoclonal antibodies and nirmatrelvir/ritonavir showed benefit. Tixagevimab/cilgavimab pre-exposure prophylaxis was recommended (PMID: 35354230).
onset_timing: Variable; risk persists beyond 12 months due to B-cell aplasia
incidence_rate: 25% mortality in CAR-T recipients with COVID-19; prolonged viral shedding median 40 days
management_protocol: Pre-exposure prophylaxis with anti-SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibodies; nirmatrelvir/ritonavir or remdesivir for acute infection; extended antiviral courses due to impaired clearance; IVIG supplementation

text_summary: In November 2023, FDA issued a class-wide safety communication regarding T-cell malignancies (including CAR-positive T-cell lymphomas) in patients who received BCMA- or CD19-directed CAR-T products. Through FAERS, 22 cases of T-cell malignancies were identified across all products, some with CAR transgene detected in the malignant T cells by PCR. The FDA updated all CAR-T product labels with a boxed warning for secondary T-cell malignancies. Estimated incidence is <1% based on ~34,000 patients treated globally (FDA Safety Communication, November 2023).
incidence_rate: <1% based on ~34,000 treated globally; 22 FAERS cases across all products as of November 2023
management_protocol: Long-term monitoring with CBC and peripheral blood flow cytometry; lymph node evaluation for unexplained lymphadenopathy; PCR for CAR transgene in suspected malignant T cells; report to FDA MedWatch

text_summary: Post-marketing surveillance identified cases of myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) following axicabtagene ciloleucel therapy. In ZUMA-1 long-term follow-up, therapy-related MDS/AML occurred in 5.5% of patients, though causality is confounded by prior alkylator and platinum exposure. CAR transgene was not detected in MDS/AML blasts, suggesting these are therapy-related rather than insertional mutagenesis events. Annual bone marrow assessment is recommended for patients with persistent cytopenias (PMID: 34543240).
incidence_rate: 5.5% therapy-related MDS/AML in ZUMA-1 long-term follow-up
management_protocol: Annual CBC with differential; bone marrow biopsy for persistent unexplained cytopenias; cytogenetics and FISH panel for MDS-associated abnormalities; referral to leukemia service if confirmed

text_summary: The FDA boxed warning for T-cell malignancies was specifically prompted by early cases identified in BCMA-directed CAR-T recipients. Among ciltacabtagene autoleucel recipients, cases of CAR-positive T-cell lymphoma were confirmed with integration site analysis demonstrating lentiviral insertion near oncogenic loci. The CARTITUDE-1 updated safety analysis reported a cumulative secondary malignancy rate of 14% at median 27.7-month follow-up, including 10% MDS/AML and individual cases of T-cell lymphoma. Ongoing FDA REMS monitoring is required (PMID: 37713520).
onset_timing: 3-24 months post-infusion for T-cell malignancies; 6-36 months for MDS/AML
incidence_rate: 14% cumulative secondary malignancies at 27.7-month follow-up; <1% CAR-positive T-cell lymphoma
management_protocol: Lifelong monitoring for secondary malignancies per REMS; peripheral blood flow cytometry q6 months; bone marrow biopsy for unexplained cytopenias; integration site analysis if T-cell malignancy suspected

text_summary: Post-marketing data from FAERS and registry studies have identified secondary primary malignancies in 3.8% of idecabtagene vicleucel recipients within 2 years. This includes therapy-related MDS/AML, non-melanoma skin cancers, and solid tumors. Distinguishing CAR-T-related from prior-therapy-related malignancies remains challenging in the heavily pretreated myeloma population. The KarMMa-3 randomized trial (ide-cel vs standard regimens) reported similar secondary malignancy rates in both arms, suggesting confounding by prior therapy exposure (PMID: 36762851).
incidence_rate: 3.8% within 2 years; similar rate in KarMMa-3 comparator arm
management_protocol: Age-appropriate cancer screening; CBC monitoring q3 months for 2 years; skin examination annually; report secondary malignancies to REMS program and FDA MedWatch

text_summary: B-cell aplasia is an expected on-target, off-tumor effect of CD19-directed CAR-T therapy and serves as a pharmacodynamic biomarker of CAR-T persistence. In ELIANA long-term data, 83% of responding patients maintained B-cell aplasia at 12 months. Resultant hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG <400 mg/dL) required immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IgRT) in 49% of patients. Subcutaneous IgG (SCIg) at 0.1 g/kg/week is preferred over IVIG for outpatient convenience. B-cell recovery with detectable CD19+ cells heralded potential relapse in some patients (PMID: 32439857).
onset_timing: Onset within 1 month; persistent in 83% at 12 months in responders
incidence_rate: 83% B-cell aplasia at 12 months; 49% requiring IgRT
management_protocol: Monthly IgG level monitoring; IgRT (IVIG 400 mg/kg q4 weeks or SCIg 0.1 g/kg/week) for IgG <400 mg/dL with recurrent infections; CD19+ B-cell monitoring for recovery; inactivated vaccines only

text_summary: Long-term immunoglobulin replacement needs following axicabtagene ciloleucel are a significant healthcare utilization concern. A CIBMTR registry analysis of 1,300 patients showed 53% had IgG <400 mg/dL at 1 year, with 38% on active IgRT at 2 years. Total annual IgRT costs averaged $40,000-$80,000 per patient. B-cell recovery occurred in 58% of patients by 2 years, but did not reliably predict freedom from IgRT. Quality-of-life impact assessments showed IgRT burden was rated as moderate-to-significant by 67% of patients (PMID: 35594413).
incidence_rate: 53% hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG <400 mg/dL) at 1 year; 38% on active IgRT at 2 years
management_protocol: IgG level monitoring q3 months; SCIg preferred for patient autonomy; trough IgG target >500 mg/dL; annual reassessment for IgRT discontinuation if B-cell recovery confirmed with adequate vaccine responses

text_summary: B-cell aplasia with lisocabtagene maraleucel showed a distinct kinetic profile in TRANSCEND NHL 001 long-term data. B-cell recovery (CD19+ >1% of lymphocytes) occurred in 60% of patients by 12 months, earlier than with axicabtagene ciloleucel or tisagenlecleucel. This may reflect the defined-composition product design allowing more controlled CAR-T persistence. Immunoglobulin replacement was required in 32% of patients. Patients with early B-cell recovery did not show worse progression-free survival, suggesting anti-tumor efficacy was maintained (PMID: 35030318).
onset_timing: B-cell aplasia onset within weeks; recovery in 60% by 12 months
incidence_rate: 60% B-cell recovery by 12 months; 32% requiring IgRT
management_protocol: Monthly B-cell and IgG monitoring; IgRT for symptomatic hypogammaglobulinemia; reassessment of IgRT need upon B-cell recovery; functional antibody response testing before discontinuing IgRT

text_summary: Cardiac toxicity with CAR-T therapy, though less common than CRS or ICANS, has emerged as a significant post-market concern. In a retrospective analysis of 137 idecabtagene vicleucel recipients, new cardiac events occurred in 12% within 6 months: atrial fibrillation (5%), heart failure/cardiomyopathy (4%), and troponin elevation (8%). Pre-existing cardiovascular disease and severe CRS were independent risk factors. Cytokine-mediated myocardial injury, particularly via IL-6 and IFN-gamma, is the proposed mechanism (PMID: 36267539).
onset_timing: Most within 14 days of CRS onset; some delayed up to 6 months
incidence_rate: 12% any cardiac event; 5% atrial fibrillation; 4% heart failure; 8% troponin elevation
management_protocol: Baseline echocardiogram and troponin; telemetry monitoring during CRS; serial troponin for grade 2+ CRS; cardiology consultation for LVEF decline >10%; beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors per heart failure guidelines

text_summary: A multi-institutional study of 470 axicabtagene ciloleucel recipients reported cardiovascular events in 17% within 1 year. Grade 3+ cardiac events occurred in 6%, including 3 cases of fatal cardiac arrest during severe CRS. Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy was identified in 1.5% of patients, presenting with acute LV dysfunction and apical ballooning during CRS. Pre-CAR-T cardiovascular risk assessment using the CHA2DS2-VASc score identified high-risk patients for enhanced cardiac monitoring (PMID: 35914404).
onset_timing: Acute events during CRS (days 2-10); chronic effects up to 12 months
incidence_rate: 17% any cardiovascular event within 1 year; 6% grade 3+; 1.5% Takotsubo cardiomyopathy
management_protocol: Baseline cardiovascular risk assessment; continuous telemetry for first 10 days; echocardiogram for unexplained tachycardia or hypotension; early tocilizumab to mitigate cytokine-driven cardiac injury

text_summary: Delayed neurotoxicity distinct from ICANS has been a class-defining safety signal for BCMA-directed CAR-T products, particularly ciltacabtagene autoleucel. In CARTITUDE-1 updated analysis, movement and neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNTs) occurred in 12% of patients: parkinsonism (5%), peripheral neuropathy (4%), cranial nerve palsy (3%), and Guillain-Barre-like syndrome (<1%). Onset ranged from 14 to 210 days post-infusion. The mechanism may involve BCMA expression on neural tissue or anti-neural antibody cross-reactivity (PMID: 37713520).
incidence_rate: 12% MNTs overall; 5% parkinsonism; 4% peripheral neuropathy; 3% cranial nerve palsy
management_protocol: Extended neurological monitoring q2 weeks for 8 weeks then monthly; MRI brain and spine for new neurological symptoms; neurology consultation mandatory; IVIG and corticosteroids for immune-mediated neuropathy; rehabilitation for persistent deficits

text_summary: Delayed neurotoxicity with idecabtagene vicleucel in KarMMa long-term data included movement and neurocognitive adverse events in 5% of patients. Presentations included peripheral neuropathy (3%), cognitive impairment (2%), and rare cases of Guillain-Barre-like syndrome. These events are distinct from acute ICANS in timing (weeks to months), phenomenology, and proposed mechanism. The FDA REMS program was updated in 2024 to require 6-month neurocognitive monitoring for all BCMA CAR-T recipients (PMID: 36762851).
onset_timing: Weeks to months post-infusion; median onset 6 weeks
incidence_rate: 5% movement and neurocognitive adverse events; 3% peripheral neuropathy; 2% cognitive impairment
management_protocol: Neurocognitive assessment (MoCA) at baseline, 1, 3, and 6 months; nerve conduction studies for neuropathy symptoms; IVIG for suspected immune-mediated neuropathy; physical and occupational therapy referral

text_summary: Brexucabtagene autoleucel for r/r ALL (ZUMA-3 trial, NCT02614066) showed a unique neurotoxicity profile. Grade 3+ ICANS occurred in 25% of adult ALL patients. Notably, 8% of patients developed delayed encephalopathy beyond day 30, with white matter changes on MRI. Seizures occurred in 5%, necessitating prophylactic levetiracetam. The higher neurotoxicity compared to MCL may reflect ALL disease burden with higher circulating blasts and greater inflammatory cytokine production (PMID: 35085378).
onset_timing: Acute ICANS: days 2-14; delayed encephalopathy: beyond day 30
incidence_rate: 25% grade 3+ ICANS; 8% delayed encephalopathy; 5% seizures
management_protocol: Mandatory seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam 500 mg BID starting day -1; EEG for suspected seizures; MRI brain for delayed neurological decline; high-dose corticosteroids and anakinra for refractory neurotoxicity

text_summary: Real-world data from the US Lymphoma CAR-T Consortium (n=275) demonstrated higher rates of severe CRS (18% grade 3+) and ICANS (31% grade 3+) with axicabtagene ciloleucel compared to the ZUMA-1 pivotal trial (13% and 28%, respectively). Older age (>65), higher ECOG performance status (2+), and elevated LDH were associated with worse toxicity in the real-world cohort. Non-relapse mortality at 1 year was 8% in real-world vs 2% in ZUMA-1, predominantly driven by infections and organ toxicity (PMID: 33956076).
onset_timing: Similar to pivotal trial; median day 2-3
incidence_rate: 18% grade 3+ CRS (real-world) vs 13% (ZUMA-1); 31% grade 3+ ICANS (real-world) vs 28% (ZUMA-1)
management_protocol: Enhanced monitoring for patients >65 years; ECOG 2+ patients require ICU-level observation; prophylactic corticosteroids considered for high-risk patients; early tocilizumab intervention

text_summary: A CIBMTR registry analysis (n=410) of tisagenlecleucel for adult DLBCL in the real-world setting showed 1-year overall survival of 48% compared to 49% in the JULIET trial, suggesting consistent efficacy. However, grade 3+ CRS was higher at 26% (vs 22% in JULIET) and infection-related mortality was 7% (vs 3%). Patients treated in the real-world were older (median 64 vs 56 years) with more comorbidities (HCT-CI score >3 in 41%). These data support label expansion considerations but highlight the need for enhanced supportive care in community settings (PMID: 34407410).
onset_timing: Similar to JULIET pivotal trial timing
incidence_rate: 26% grade 3+ CRS in real-world vs 22% in JULIET; 7% infection-related mortality vs 3%
management_protocol: Pre-infusion comorbidity assessment with HCT-CI scoring; certified CAR-T center requirements; enhanced antimicrobial prophylaxis for older patients; telemedicine follow-up protocols for community patients

text_summary: Early real-world experience with lisocabtagene maraleucel from 12 US academic centers (n=196) confirmed the favorable safety profile seen in TRANSCEND NHL 001. Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 3% and grade 3+ ICANS in 8%, comparable to pivotal trial rates (2% and 10%). Outpatient CAR-T administration was feasible in 32% of patients, with 87% of outpatient-treated patients avoiding ICU admission. The defined-composition product enabled more predictable toxicity profiles and outpatient administration protocols (PMID: 36481667).
onset_timing: Consistent with pivotal trial; median day 5
incidence_rate: 3% grade 3+ CRS (real-world) vs 2% (pivotal); 8% grade 3+ ICANS (real-world) vs 10% (pivotal)
management_protocol: Outpatient administration feasible for low-risk patients (low tumor burden, ECOG 0-1, no comorbidities); telemedicine check-ins q12h; same-day readmission pathway for grade 2+ CRS

text_summary: Real-world data for ciltacabtagene autoleucel from the CARTITUDE-US expanded access program (n=88) demonstrated grade 3+ CRS in 2.3% and delayed neurotoxicity (MNTs) in 8%, comparable to CARTITUDE-1 (4% and 12%, respectively). However, the real-world population included patients with lower ECOG scores and fewer prior lines of therapy due to the CARTITUDE-4 frontline data. Outpatient monitoring after day 10 was feasible in 55% of patients. Prolonged cytopenias (>60 days) remained a significant challenge at 36% (PMID: 37925840).
onset_timing: Median day 7, consistent with pivotal trial
incidence_rate: 2.3% grade 3+ CRS; 8% MNTs; 36% prolonged cytopenias >60 days in real-world setting
management_protocol: Outpatient monitoring feasible after day 10 for selected patients; weekly neurocognitive screening for 8 weeks; extended cytopenia monitoring and supportive care

text_summary: All six FDA-approved CAR-T products are subject to Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) programs requiring certified healthcare facilities and providers. REMS elements include: (1) prescriber training on CRS and ICANS recognition and management, (2) tocilizumab availability within 2 hours at the treatment site, (3) patient monitoring for 7+ days post-infusion, (4) patient wallet card with emergency information. As of 2024, 200+ sites are REMS-certified in the US. REMS audits show 98% compliance with tocilizumab availability and 94% with monitoring requirements.
incidence_rate: N/A - applies to all treated patients
management_protocol: REMS-certified center requirements: trained physician and healthcare team; minimum 2 doses tocilizumab on-site; ICU access; patient monitoring for minimum 7 days (Yescarta), 7 days (Tecartus), 7 days (Kymriah); patient counseling and wallet card

text_summary: In January 2024, FDA mandated updated boxed warnings for all approved CAR-T products to include T-cell malignancies in addition to existing CRS and neurological toxicity warnings. The label update followed identification of 33 cases of T-cell malignancy (including T-cell lymphoma and T-cell leukemia) across all 6 products through FAERS, some confirmed CAR-positive by PCR. The FDA concluded the benefit-risk remains favorable but requires lifelong malignancy monitoring. Product labels now mandate reporting of all secondary T-cell malignancies to the manufacturer and FDA.
incidence_rate: 33 cases across ~34,000 total patients treated globally (<0.1%)
management_protocol: Lifelong monitoring for secondary malignancies; annual CBC, peripheral smear, and LDH; flow cytometry for unexplained lymphocytosis; biopsy of suspicious lesions with CAR transgene PCR analysis; reporting to manufacturer and FDA MedWatch within 15 days

text_summary: The 2019 ASTCT consensus grading for CRS and ICANS (Lee et al.) standardized adverse event reporting across CAR-T products, replacing product-specific grading scales (Penn, Lee, CTCAE). CRS grading: Grade 1 (fever only), Grade 2 (hypotension responding to fluids or low-dose vasopressors; O2 <40%), Grade 3 (hypotension requiring high-dose vasopressors; O2 >=40%), Grade 4 (life-threatening, ventilator required). ICANS grading based on ICE score: Grade 1 (ICE 7-9), Grade 2 (ICE 3-6), Grade 3 (ICE 0-2), Grade 4 (obtunded). All post-2019 labels adopted ASTCT grading (PMID: 30442462).
incidence_rate: N/A - standardized grading applicable to all CAR-T products
management_protocol: ASTCT consensus grading applied uniformly: Grade 1 CRS - supportive care; Grade 2 - tocilizumab; Grade 3 - tocilizumab + corticosteroids + ICU; Grade 4 - tocilizumab + high-dose corticosteroids + vasopressors + ventilation. ICANS: Grade 1 - monitoring; Grade 2 - dexamethasone; Grade 3-4 - methylprednisolone 1g IV

text_summary: FDA required a class-wide label update in March 2024 strengthening warnings for prolonged and recurrent cytopenias across all CAR-T products. The updated Warnings and Precautions section now includes recommended monitoring: CBC with differential at baseline, weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly for 6 months. The update was prompted by post-marketing reports of fatal infections secondary to prolonged neutropenia and cases of aplastic anemia requiring stem cell rescue. Additionally, labels were updated to recommend hematology consultation for cytopenias persisting beyond 90 days (FDA Label Supplement, March 2024).
onset_timing: Persistent beyond 30 days per updated label definition
incidence_rate: 30-40% prolonged cytopenias across products; rare aplastic anemia requiring stem cell rescue
management_protocol: CBC with differential: weekly x4 weeks then monthly x6 months per updated label; G-CSF per institutional guidelines; hematology referral for cytopenias >90 days; bone marrow biopsy to assess cellularity; autologous or allogeneic stem cell rescue for refractory aplasia

text_summary: Cardiac toxicity with tisagenlecleucel was observed in a post-marketing analysis of 312 pediatric and adult recipients. Troponin elevation (>0.04 ng/mL) occurred in 14% of patients, typically concurrent with grade 2+ CRS. Grade 2 troponin elevation (>10x ULN) was identified in 6% of cases with associated transient left ventricular dysfunction (LVEF decline >10% from baseline). Two pediatric patients developed acute myocarditis requiring inotropic support. Pre-existing cardiac conditions and peak IL-6 levels >1000 pg/mL were independent risk factors. All cardiac events resolved with CRS management and supportive cardiology care (PMID: 36267539).
onset_timing: Concurrent with CRS, typically days 3-8 post-infusion
incidence_rate: 14% troponin elevation; 6% grade 2 with LV dysfunction
management_protocol: Baseline troponin and echocardiogram pre-infusion; serial troponin q12h during grade 2+ CRS; echocardiogram for troponin >10x ULN; cardiology consultation for LVEF decline; early tocilizumab to reduce cytokine-mediated myocardial injury

text_summary: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) as a complication of severe CRS was reported in a retrospective analysis of 189 axicabtagene ciloleucel recipients across 5 academic centers. Grade 3 coagulopathy/DIC occurred in 4.8% of patients, exclusively in the setting of grade 3-4 CRS. Laboratory features included: fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, D-dimer >10x ULN, PT/INR prolongation, and thrombocytopenia (<20,000/uL). Two patients required cryoprecipitate transfusion and one required fresh frozen plasma. DIC resolved in all cases with CRS management via tocilizumab and corticosteroids. Elevated ferritin >10,000 ng/mL and IL-6 >5000 pg/mL at CRS onset were predictive of subsequent DIC development.
onset_timing: Concurrent with grade 3-4 CRS; median day 4 post-infusion
incidence_rate: 4.8% grade 3 DIC; exclusively with severe CRS
management_protocol: Fibrinogen monitoring q6h during grade 3+ CRS; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <150 mg/dL; FFP for INR >2.0; platelet transfusion for <20K with active bleeding; aggressive CRS management with tocilizumab and corticosteroids; hematology consultation

text_summary: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel-associated movement and neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNTs) represent a unique delayed neurotoxicity pattern distinct from ICANS. In a pooled analysis of CARTITUDE-1 and CARTITUDE-4 (n=285), parkinsonian features (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, masked facies) occurred in 6.3% of patients with median onset 42 days post-infusion (range 14-210 days). MRI brain was typically unremarkable. DaTscan showed reduced dopaminergic transporter uptake in 3/7 evaluated patients. Response to levodopa/carbidopa was variable (partial improvement in 40%). Pathophysiology hypothesized to involve BCMA cross-reactivity with basal ganglia structures or anti-neuronal antibody-mediated injury. Two fatal cases were attributed to progressive MNT syndrome.
onset_timing: Median 42 days post-infusion (range 14-210 days); delayed onset distinct from acute ICANS
incidence_rate: 6.3% parkinsonian features; 4% peripheral neuropathy; 3% cranial nerve palsy in pooled CARTITUDE analysis
management_protocol: Extended neurological monitoring q2 weeks for 12 weeks post-infusion; DaTscan for parkinsonian symptoms; levodopa/carbidopa trial; IVIG 0.4 g/kg x5 days for suspected immune-mediated process; physical and occupational therapy; corticosteroids for progressive symptoms

text_summary: Cardiac arrhythmias with lisocabtagene maraleucel were identified in a post-marketing analysis of 425 recipients across the TRANSCEND program and real-world use. New-onset atrial fibrillation occurred in 7.1% (grade 2-3), ventricular tachycardia in 1.6% (grade 3), and QTc prolongation >500ms in 2.8% (grade 2-3). Arrhythmias occurred predominantly during CRS (82% of cases) with median onset day 6 post-infusion. Pre-existing cardiac history (coronary artery disease, prior arrhythmia, structural heart disease) was present in 65% of affected patients. One case of torsades de pointes with concurrent electrolyte abnormalities required cardioversion. All arrhythmias resolved with standard antiarrhythmic management and CRS resolution.
onset_timing: Median day 6 post-infusion; predominantly during CRS
incidence_rate: 7.1% atrial fibrillation; 1.6% ventricular tachycardia; 2.8% QTc prolongation
management_protocol: Baseline ECG and echocardiogram; continuous telemetry for 10 days post-infusion; electrolyte monitoring (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) q12h during CRS; QTc-prolonging medications hold list; cardiology consultation for new arrhythmia; amiodarone or beta-blocker per arrhythmia type

text_summary: A case report documented CAR-positive T-cell lymphoma arising 14 months after idecabtagene vicleucel infusion for relapsed multiple myeloma. The patient presented with rapidly progressive lymphadenopathy and elevated LDH. Biopsy revealed anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL) morphology with surface CAR expression confirmed by flow cytometry using anti-idiotype antibody. PCR detected the CAR transgene in tumor cells. Lentiviral integration site analysis by LIAM-PCR identified a clonal integration near the TNFRSF8 (CD30) locus. The patient received brentuximab vedotin with partial response. This case contributed to the FDA boxed warning update for secondary T-cell malignancies across all CAR-T products.
incidence_rate: Case report; estimated <0.05% incidence across all CAR-T products
management_protocol: Biopsy with CAR transgene PCR and integration site analysis; lentiviral-specific PCR to confirm CAR-positive malignancy; treatment per lymphoma subtype; brentuximab vedotin for CD30+ cases; report to FDA MedWatch and manufacturer REMS program

text_summary: FDA postmarket surveillance identified 8 cases of T-cell malignancy among approximately 12,000 axicabtagene ciloleucel recipients through December 2023. Cases included: 4 T-cell lymphomas (3 confirmed CAR-positive by PCR), 2 T-cell large granular lymphocyte leukemia, and 2 peripheral T-cell lymphoma NOS. Median time to diagnosis was 16 months (range 2-60 months). CAR transgene was detected in malignant cells in 5/8 cases, with integration site analysis showing proximity to known oncogenes (PBX1, LMO2) in 3 cases. Despite these events, FDA concluded benefit-risk remains favorable given <0.07% incidence vs >50% response rates. Long-term follow-up studies (15-year mandated by FDA) are ongoing.
incidence_rate: 8 cases per ~12,000 treated (<0.07%); 5/8 CAR-positive by PCR
management_protocol: 15-year FDA-mandated post-infusion follow-up; annual CBC with differential and peripheral blood flow cytometry; lymph node biopsy for unexplained lymphadenopathy; CAR transgene PCR on any suspected T-cell neoplasm; integration site analysis via LIAM-PCR

text_summary: Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS)-associated acute kidney injury (AKI) has been reported across CAR-T products, primarily in patients with high tumor burden. In a multicenter analysis of 542 CAR-T recipients (all products), grade 3 AKI occurred in 5.2% within 14 days of infusion. TLS was confirmed in 3.1% with hyperuricemia (>8 mg/dL), hyperphosphatemia, hyperkalemia, and hypocalcemia. Patients with LDH >2x ULN and bulky disease (>7.5 cm) were at highest risk (12.3% AKI incidence). Two patients required temporary hemodialysis. Prophylactic rasburicase and aggressive IV hydration (3 L/m2/day) reduced AKI incidence to 1.8% in a subsequent protocol-amended cohort.
onset_timing: Days 2-10 post-infusion; concurrent with CRS and tumor lysis
incidence_rate: 5.2% grade 3 AKI; 3.1% confirmed TLS in high tumor burden patients
management_protocol: Pre-infusion risk stratification by LDH and tumor bulk; prophylactic rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg for high-risk patients; IV hydration 3 L/m2/day; uric acid, phosphorus, potassium, calcium monitoring q6h for 72h; nephrology consultation for creatinine >3x baseline; hemodialysis availability

text_summary: Prolonged cytopenias exceeding 90 days post-infusion were particularly severe with brexucabtagene autoleucel in the ZUMA-3 trial for r/r ALL. Grade 3-4 neutropenia persisted beyond 90 days in 19% of patients, with thrombocytopenia in 15% and pancytopenia in 8%. Three patients required autologous stem cell rescue for refractory aplasia. The heavily pretreated ALL population (median 3 prior lines including allogeneic SCT in 35%) had severely compromised marrow reserve. Bone marrow biopsy at day 90 in affected patients showed hypocellular marrow (<20% cellularity) with fibrosis in 60% of cases. G-CSF and TPO-agonist combination therapy improved neutrophil and platelet counts in 65% of patients within 30 days of initiation.
incidence_rate: 19% grade 3-4 neutropenia >90 days; 15% thrombocytopenia >90 days; 8% pancytopenia
management_protocol: Bone marrow biopsy at day 90 for persistent cytopenias; G-CSF + TPO-agonist (romiplostim 3-10 mcg/kg/week) combination; antimicrobial prophylaxis escalation; autologous stem cell rescue consideration for refractory aplasia; hematology review at day 60, 90, 120

text_summary: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel exhibited a unique delayed CRS pattern in CARTITUDE-1 and CARTITUDE-4, with CRS onset on day 7-14 in 38% of patients, significantly later than other CAR-T products (typically day 1-3). A biphasic CRS pattern was observed in 12% of patients: initial low-grade fever (grade 1) on days 1-3 that resolved, followed by a second, more severe CRS episode on days 7-14 requiring tocilizumab. This delayed pattern is attributed to the dual VHH nanobody's progressive tumor engagement kinetics. Late-onset CRS was associated with higher peak CAR-T expansion (median 1200 vs 650 copies/ug DNA) and deeper clinical responses (92% vs 78% sCR). Outpatient monitoring strategies require extended observation windows for Carvykti recipients.
severity_grade: Any grade (95%); Delayed grade 2-3 onset day 7-14 in 38%
onset_timing: Biphasic: initial grade 1 days 1-3; delayed escalation days 7-14 in 38%
incidence_rate: 38% with CRS onset day 7-14; 12% biphasic pattern; 4% grade 3-4 overall
management_protocol: Extended inpatient observation minimum 10 days for Carvykti; daily fever and CRS assessment through day 14; tocilizumab available on-site for 14 days post-infusion; patient education on delayed CRS symptoms for post-discharge monitoring; readmission pathway for late fever

text_summary: Hepatotoxicity with tisagenlecleucel was characterized in a post-marketing analysis of 287 recipients. Transaminase elevation (ALT/AST >3x ULN) occurred in 21% of patients, with grade 2-3 hepatotoxicity in 12%. Timing was biphasic: early hepatotoxicity (days 1-7) correlated with lymphodepletion chemotherapy (fludarabine/cyclophosphamide), while late hepatotoxicity (days 7-28) correlated with CRS severity and peak IL-6 levels. Grade 3 transaminase elevation (>5x ULN) occurred in 4.5% of patients, with concurrent hyperbilirubinemia in 2.1%. Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD) was reported in 3 patients (1%) with prior hepatotoxic therapy exposure. All cases resolved with CRS management and ursodiol prophylaxis in SOS-risk patients.
incidence_rate: 21% transaminase elevation; 12% grade 2-3; 4.5% grade 3; 1% SOS/VOD
management_protocol: Baseline LFTs and hepatitis screening; LFT monitoring q48h during CRS; hold hepatotoxic medications; ursodiol 300 mg TID prophylaxis for SOS-risk patients; hepatology consultation for bilirubin >3x ULN; N-acetylcysteine consideration for severe transaminase elevation

text_summary: Fatal cerebral edema is the most feared complication of ICANS, occurring in <1% of all CAR-T recipients but carrying >80% mortality when it develops. An analysis of 14 fatal cerebral edema cases across all approved CAR-T products (2017-2023) identified common features: rapid ICE score decline from 10 to 0 within 12 hours, diffuse cerebral edema on CT/MRI, and failure to respond to high-dose corticosteroids. Peak serum IL-2 and IL-15 levels were >50x higher than in non-fatal ICANS cases. Proposed mechanism involves massive endothelial activation with blood-brain barrier disruption, astrocyte dysfunction, and excitotoxic glutamate release. Early aggressive intervention with high-dose methylprednisolone (1g IV daily x3 days) at first sign of rapidly progressive ICANS may reduce mortality, though prospective data are limited.
onset_timing: Typically days 3-7 post-infusion; rapid progression over 12-24 hours
incidence_rate: <1% across all products; >80% mortality when fully developed
management_protocol: ICE score monitoring q4h for grade 2+ ICANS; emergent CT head for ICE score <3 or rapid decline; high-dose methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3 for suspected cerebral edema; neurosurgery consultation for ICP monitoring; mannitol/hypertonic saline for ICP management; intubation and hyperventilation for herniation signs; siltuximab or anakinra for steroid-refractory cases

text_summary: In November 2023, the FDA issued a landmark safety communication mandating a new boxed warning for secondary T-cell malignancies across all six approved CAR-T cell products (Kymriah, Yescarta, Tecartus, Breyanzi, Abecma, Carvykti). The action was triggered by 22 confirmed cases of T-cell malignancies identified through FAERS, including T-cell lymphoma, T-cell leukemia, and other T-cell neoplasms. CAR transgene was identified in the malignant cells in a subset of cases. The FDA concluded that the risk is class-wide and not product-specific, as cases spanned lentiviral and retroviral vector platforms, CD19 and BCMA targets, and both 4-1BB and CD28 costimulatory domains. The FDA continues to assess the overall risk-benefit, which remains favorable given <0.1% incidence versus substantial clinical benefit in otherwise fatal malignancies.
incidence_rate: 22 cases across ~34,000 treated globally (<0.1%) as of November 2023; updated to 33 cases by January 2024
management_protocol: Lifelong monitoring for all CAR-T recipients; annual CBC with differential, peripheral blood smear, and LDH; lymph node exam q6 months for 5 years; biopsy of unexplained lymphadenopathy with CAR transgene PCR; mandatory reporting to FDA MedWatch within 15 days; REMS program compliance for monitoring and reporting

text_summary: T-cell lymphoma following CAR-T therapy: FDA-commissioned analysis of FAERS data identified 33 cases of T-cell malignancy among approximately 34,000 commercial CAR-T recipients through mid-2025 (incidence 0.10%, 95% CI 0.07-0.14%). Cases occurred at median 14 months post-infusion (range 2-60 months). Product distribution: axi-cel 0.12%, tisa-cel 0.08%, liso-cel 0.09%, ide-cel 0.11%, cilta-cel 0.07%. Vector integration site analysis available in 21 cases: 14 showed CAR transgene near known oncogenes (TET2, DNMT3A, TP53 pathway). Risk factors: prior alkylating agents, pre-existing clonal hematopoiesis, possibly higher VCN. FDA concluded benefit-risk remains favorable but mandated enhanced 15-year monitoring. Boxed warning added November 2023 to all CAR-T labels.
management_protocol: Enhanced long-term monitoring; molecular characterization of any new lymphoproliferative disorder; report to manufacturer and FDA MedWatch; consider early biopsy of any suspicious lymphadenopathy
outcome: 12 deaths, 8 CR after treatment, 13 ongoing treatment

text_summary: Delayed neurotoxicity (parkinsonism and movement disorders) after cilta-cel: Observed in 5.0% of patients in CARTITUDE-4 and 4.2% across all cilta-cel trials. Onset typically 20-120 days post-infusion. Symptoms include parkinsonian features (bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor), cranial nerve palsies, peripheral neuropathy, and cognitive changes. Distinct from ICANS which occurs in first 1-2 weeks. Proposed mechanism: CAR-T cells crossing BBB with sustained activation against BCMA-expressing cells in CNS niches. Management: high-dose corticosteroids (early intervention), physical/occupational therapy, dopaminergic agents for parkinsonism. Full recovery in 30-40%; partial recovery 40-50%; persistent deficit 10-20%. Risk factors: high tumor burden, high-grade CRS, >3 prior lines.
incidence_rate: 4.2-5.0% across cilta-cel trials
management_protocol: Early high-dose corticosteroids (dexamethasone 40mg daily); neurology consult; MRI brain; dopaminergic agents for parkinsonism; physical/occupational therapy; speech therapy if cranial nerves affected

text_summary: Immune reconstitution after CD19 CAR-T: Prospective monitoring of 248 DLBCL patients. B-cell recovery (CD19+ >50/uL) at median 7 months (range 3-24). IgG <400 mg/dL requiring IVIG in 42% at 6 months, 28% at 12 months, 15% at 24 months. CD4 count recovery to >200/uL at median 6 months. Infections (any grade): 32% in months 0-3, 18% in months 3-6, 12% in months 6-12. Vaccine titers: influenza response 45% at 12 months; pneumococcal response 32% at 12 months (inactivated vaccines only). Live vaccines contraindicated for ≥12 months. Recommendation: monthly IgG monitoring, IVIG if <400, antimicrobial prophylaxis (acyclovir, fluconazole, TMP-SMX) for minimum 6 months.
incidence_rate: Hypogammaglobulinemia 42% at 6mo, infections 32% at 0-3mo
management_protocol: Monthly IgG monitoring; IVIG replacement if IgG<400; prophylaxis: acyclovir 400mg BID (HSV/VZV), fluconazole 200mg daily (fungal), TMP-SMX 3x/week (PJP); inactivated vaccines at 6-12 months post CAR-T
outcome: Immune reconstitution in majority by 12-24 months

text_summary: Anakinra for CRS management: Prospective comparison of tocilizumab vs anakinra for grade ≥2 CRS in 186 patients. Anakinra (IV 200mg q8h or subcutaneous 100mg daily) resolved grade 2 CRS in 89% without corticosteroids (vs 82% with tocilizumab). For grade 3-4 CRS with HLH/MAS features (ferritin >10,000, rising triglycerides, falling fibrinogen): anakinra superior to tocilizumab (resolution 78% vs 52%, p=0.008). Anakinra does not mask IL-6 levels (critical for monitoring), unlike tocilizumab which causes paradoxical IL-6 elevation. Recommended approach: tocilizumab first-line for grade 2 CRS, add anakinra for refractory CRS or if HLH/MAS features present. Consider anakinra first-line when ferritin >5000 at CRS onset.
incidence_rate: Grade ≥2 CRS: 15-30% across products
management_protocol: Grade 2: tocilizumab 8mg/kg IV (max 800mg), may repeat x1 in 8h; add anakinra 200mg IV q8h if refractory or ferritin >5000; Grade 3-4: tocilizumab + dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h + anakinra; HLH/MAS features: anakinra first-line + ruxolitinib 10mg BID; refractory: emapalumab (anti-IFN-gamma)
outcome: CRS resolution in >95% with combined approach

text_summary: ICANS management guidelines 2024 ASTCT update: Grading by ICE score (10-point). Grade 1 (ICE 7-9): observe, supportive care. Grade 2 (ICE 3-6): dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h, continuous EEG monitoring if available, neurology consult. Grade 3 (ICE 0-2): dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h, ICU transfer, seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam 500mg BID), consider anti-IL-1 (anakinra) or anti-IL-6 (siltuximab, NOT tocilizumab if concurrent CRS). Grade 4 (patient obtunded, seizures, cerebral edema): high-dose methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3, consider intrathecal cytarabine if refractory. New recommendation: prophylactic corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10mg pre-infusion + day 0-2) reduced grade ≥3 ICANS from 15% to 5% in high-risk patients (high LDH, CNS involvement) without impacting CAR-T efficacy.
incidence_rate: Any grade: 20-65% by product; grade ≥3: 10-25%
management_protocol: Grade-based management per 2024 ASTCT guidelines; ICE scoring q6-12h during risk period; seizure prophylaxis for grade ≥2; EEG monitoring; avoid intrathecal therapy unless grade 4 refractory to steroids; prophylactic dexamethasone for high-risk patients
outcome: Full resolution in >90%; persistent cognitive changes rare (<5%)

text_summary: Prolonged cytopenias management with TPO receptor agonists: Retrospective analysis of 312 patients with grade ≥3 cytopenias persisting >30 days post CAR-T. Eltrombopag (50-150mg daily) started at day 30 for platelets <50K: platelet recovery to >50K at median 18 days (vs 42 days observation, p<0.001). Romiplostim (3-5 mcg/kg weekly) equally effective. G-CSF for ANC <500: recovery at median 7 days (vs 14 days without, p=0.003). Recommended approach: G-CSF at day 14 if ANC <500; eltrombopag at day 30 if platelets <50K; bone marrow biopsy at day 60 if persistent. Risk factors: prior HSCT, CRS grade ≥2, baseline cytopenias, >4 prior lines.
incidence_rate: Grade 3+: 30-60% at day 30; persistent >90 days: 5-15%
management_protocol: G-CSF 5mcg/kg daily if ANC <500 after day 14; eltrombopag 50-150mg daily or romiplostim 3-5mcg/kg weekly if platelets <50K after day 30; transfusion support (RBC if Hgb<7, platelets if <10K or bleeding); bone marrow biopsy if persistent >60 days; iron studies, B12, folate
outcome: Recovery in 85% by day 90 with TPO-RA support

text_summary: GPRC5D CAR-T nail and skin toxicity: Observed in 40-70% of patients receiving GPRC5D-targeted therapies (CAR-T and bispecific antibodies). Nail changes (onycholysis, ridging, discoloration) in 47%, onset 2-8 weeks. Skin dryness/rash in 34%, onset 1-4 weeks. Dysgeusia (taste changes) in 44%, onset 1-2 weeks. All reflect GPRC5D expression on hair follicles and skin. Grade 3+ nail/skin toxicity in 5-8%. Management: emollients for skin dryness, nail hardeners, biotin supplementation, taste modification counseling. All reversible upon CAR-T exhaustion (typically 3-6 months). Dose-response relationship observed: higher CAR-T expansion correlates with more severe skin/nail changes.
incidence_rate: Nail 47%, skin 34%, dysgeusia 44%
management_protocol: Nail: gentle nail care, biotin 5mg daily, avoid trauma; Skin: emollients TID, mild topical corticosteroids for rash; Dysgeusia: zinc supplementation, dietary counseling; All: self-resolving in 3-6 months as CAR-T exhausts

text_summary: GD2 CAR-T neuropathic pain syndrome: On-target/off-tumor toxicity from GD2 expression on peripheral nerves and CNS neurons. Observed in 30-60% of patients. Onset: days 1-14 post-infusion. Characteristics: severe lancinating pain in extremities, allodynia, hyperalgesia. Can mimic Guillain-Barré syndrome in severe cases. Grade 3+ pain in 15-25%. Management: gabapentin 300-900mg TID, pregabalin 75-150mg BID, opioids for severe breakthrough pain, IV lidocaine for refractory cases. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10mg) if grade ≥3 or motor component. Duration: typically 1-3 weeks, self-resolving as CAR-T levels decline. Risk factors: higher dose, higher expansion, prior neurotoxic therapy.
incidence_rate: 30-60% any grade; 15-25% grade 3+
management_protocol: Gabapentin 300-900mg TID escalating; pregabalin 75-150mg BID if gabapentin inadequate; opioids for breakthrough; IV lidocaine 1-2mg/kg/hr for refractory; corticosteroids if motor component or grade ≥3

text_summary: Hepatotoxicity with GPC3-targeted CAR-T: Expected on-target toxicity from GPC3 expression on normal hepatocytes (low level) and fetal liver. In Phase II GPC3 CAR-T studies, AST/ALT elevation: any grade 58%, grade ≥3 22%, grade 4 (ALT >20x ULN) 3%. Bilirubin elevation grade ≥3 in 8%. Onset: days 3-14 (often coincides with CRS). Hepatic artery infusion delivery increases local hepatic toxicity. Management: daily LFTs during CRS period, N-acetylcysteine if ALT >10x ULN, high-dose corticosteroids if immune-mediated pattern, hepatology consult for grade ≥3. Differentiate from CRS-associated transaminitis (usually milder, resolves with CRS treatment) vs direct CAR-T hepatotoxicity (can persist).
incidence_rate: Any grade 58%, grade ≥3 22%
management_protocol: Daily LFTs during CRS period; NAC if ALT >10x ULN; corticosteroids if immune-mediated (methylprednisolone 2mg/kg); hepatology consult grade ≥3; differentiate from CRS-associated transaminitis; hold further CAR-T dosing if grade 4
outcome: Resolution in >90% by day 30; 3% required intensive management

text_summary: DIC/coagulopathy during severe CRS: Disseminated intravascular coagulation occurs in 5-15% of patients with severe CRS (grade ≥3). Subclinical DIC (elevated D-dimer without overt bleeding) in up to 30%. Mechanism: massive cytokine release triggers endothelial activation and coagulation cascade. Key biomarkers: D-dimer >10x ULN, falling fibrinogen (<150 mg/dL), prolonged PT/INR, thrombocytopenia. Management: treat underlying CRS aggressively (tocilizumab ± steroids); cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100; FFP for active bleeding with prolonged PT; platelet transfusion if <20K or active bleeding; heparin controversial (generally avoid). Monitor fibrinogen q6h during grade ≥3 CRS. Resolution parallels CRS resolution.
incidence_rate: 5-15% overt DIC with grade ≥3 CRS; subclinical 30%
management_protocol: Treat underlying CRS first; fibrinogen monitoring q6h during grade ≥3 CRS; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100; FFP for PT >1.5x ULN with bleeding; platelet transfusion if <20K; avoid heparin; consult hematology
outcome: Resolves with CRS in >95%; fatal hemorrhage <1%

text_summary: HLH/MAS differentiation from CRS and specific management: HLH/MAS occurs in 1-5% of CAR-T recipients, often as escalation of severe CRS. Key differentiators from CRS: ferritin >10,000 (97% sensitivity for MAS), falling fibrinogen (opposite of rising in CRS), rising triglycerides, cytopenias worsening despite CRS treatment, hepatosplenomegaly, bone marrow hemophagocytosis. ASTCT recommends HLH/MAS screening when ferritin >10,000 during CRS. Management differs from CRS: anakinra preferred over tocilizumab (IL-1 blockade more effective in MAS); ruxolitinib 10mg BID as second-line; emapalumab (anti-IFN-gamma) for refractory; etoposide only as last resort. Avoid tocilizumab monotherapy for MAS (may worsen hepatic involvement). Mortality 10-20% for CAR-T-associated HLH/MAS.
incidence_rate: 1-5% of all CAR-T recipients
management_protocol: Anakinra 200mg IV q8h (first-line for MAS, not tocilizumab); ruxolitinib 10mg BID if refractory; emapalumab 6mg/kg for refractory; etoposide 100mg/m2 as last resort; high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1g x3); daily ferritin, fibrinogen, triglycerides, LDH monitoring

text_summary: Cardiac toxicity monitoring protocol post CAR-T: Troponin elevation (any grade) in 10-25% of CAR-T recipients, usually during CRS. Clinically significant cardiac events (arrhythmia, LV dysfunction) in 2-5%. Pre-existing cardiac disease increases risk. Recommended monitoring: baseline troponin and BNP pre-infusion; troponin daily during CRS (days 0-14); BNP if troponin elevated; echocardiogram if troponin >3x ULN or new symptoms; 12-lead ECG daily during hospitalization. Management of troponin elevation: cardiology consult if >3x ULN; continuous telemetry; treat underlying CRS; consider holding tocilizumab if cardiogenic shock (switch to siltuximab). Long-term: LVEF monitoring at 3, 6, 12 months if significant troponin elevation.
incidence_rate: Troponin elevation 10-25%, clinical cardiac events 2-5%
management_protocol: Baseline troponin + BNP; daily troponin during CRS; echo if troponin >3x ULN; cardiology consult grade ≥3; continuous telemetry during CRS; treat underlying CRS; long-term LVEF monitoring at 3/6/12 months if significant elevation
outcome: Resolution in >90% with CRS treatment; persistent LV dysfunction <2%

text_summary: Elderly-specific toxicity data (≥75 years): Multicenter analysis of 284 patients ≥75 years receiving commercial CAR-T. Compared to younger cohort (n=1,420, age <65): CRS any grade similar (85% vs 82%); CRS grade ≥3 higher (8% vs 5%, p=0.04); ICANS grade ≥3 higher (15% vs 10%, p=0.02); ICU admission higher (28% vs 18%, p<0.001); non-relapse mortality at 1 year higher (8% vs 3%, p<0.001). Infections more common: 42% vs 28% in first 90 days. Prolonged cytopenias: 45% vs 32% at day 30. However, patients with ECOG 0-1 regardless of age had comparable toxicity rates. Conclusion: functional status, not chronological age, should guide CAR-T eligibility. Geriatric assessment (CGA) recommended for patients ≥70.
incidence_rate: CRS grade ≥3: 8% (vs 5% younger); ICANS grade ≥3: 15% (vs 10%); ICU 28%; NRM 8%
management_protocol: Geriatric assessment pre-CAR-T; ECOG 0-1 required for optimal outcomes; enhanced monitoring (daily labs through day 14); lower threshold for ICU transfer; proactive infection prophylaxis; early PT/OT
outcome: Outcomes comparable to younger if ECOG 0-1; higher NRM if ECOG ≥2

text_summary: Post CAR-T vaccination schedule: Consensus recommendations from ASH/EBMT/FACT. Timeline assumes B-cell recovery (CD19+ >10/uL) and IgG >400 without IVIG. Inactivated vaccines (start at 6 months post CAR-T if B-cell recovery): influenza (annually), COVID-19 (per current CDC), pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23), Tdap, hepatitis B series, meningococcal. Live vaccines (start at ≥12 months post CAR-T AND confirmed immune reconstitution AND off immunosuppression): varicella/zoster (recombinant Shingrix preferred over live Zostavax), MMR (if seronegative). Check vaccine titers 4-8 weeks post vaccination; revaccinate if non-protective. Continue antimicrobial prophylaxis until demonstrated vaccine responses. IVIG recipients: hold IVIG for 2 weeks before and after vaccination to avoid interference.
incidence_rate: N/A (prophylaxis protocol)
management_protocol: 6 months: inactivated influenza, COVID-19, PCV20, Tdap; 9 months: hepatitis B series, meningococcal; 12 months: check all titers, revaccinate non-responders; ≥12 months: recombinant zoster vaccine if eligible; Live vaccines only after confirmed immune reconstitution; Hold IVIG 2 weeks before/after vaccines
outcome: Protective titers achieved in 45-65% at 12 months for most antigens

text_summary: COVID-19 outcomes in CAR-T recipients: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 2,847 CAR-T recipients with COVID-19 (2020-2025). Overall mortality 12.3% (vs 1.1% general population, vs 3.8% in solid tumor patients). Mortality by vaccination status: unvaccinated 24.1%, partially vaccinated 14.2%, fully vaccinated 8.7%, boosted 5.1%. Risk factors: within 90 days of CAR-T (mortality 28%), hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG<400, mortality 18%), ongoing cytopenias (mortality 15%). Antibody response to COVID vaccination was poor: seroconversion 32% after primary series (vs >95% immunocompetent). T-cell responses preserved in 65%. Pre-exposure prophylaxis (tixagevimab/cilgavimab or equivalent monoclonal) reduced hospitalization by 72%. Current recommendation: pre-exposure prophylaxis for all CAR-T recipients in first 12 months; monitor for emerging variants.
onset_timing: Any time post-infusion; highest risk <90 days
incidence_rate: 12.3% mortality; seroconversion 32%
management_protocol: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (monoclonal antibody); early antiviral treatment (Paxlovid) at symptom onset; COVID vaccination (reduced but non-zero benefit); monitor IgG and consider IVIG; isolation precautions during high community transmission
outcome: Mortality declining with vaccination + antivirals (from 24% to 5%)

text_summary: GVHD with allogeneic CAR-T products: Pooled safety analysis of 312 patients across allogeneic CAR-T trials (CTX110, ALLO-501A, UCART19, FT819). Overall GvHD incidence: 3.2% (10/312). Acute GvHD only (no chronic observed). Grade 1-2 GvHD: 2.6%; grade 3-4: 0.6%. Products with TRAC knockout (CTX110, FT819): GvHD 0% (0/142). Products with TALEN TCR editing (ALLO-501A, UCART19): GvHD 5.9% (10/170). Difference likely reflects higher residual TCR+ cell contamination with TALEN vs CRISPR approaches. GvHD onset: median day 28 (range 14-56). Management: corticosteroids first-line, all responded. No GvHD-related deaths. Improved gene editing technologies are expected to further reduce residual TCR+ contamination.
incidence_rate: 3.2% overall; 0% with CRISPR TRAC KO; 5.9% with TALEN
management_protocol: Corticosteroids first-line; ruxolitinib for steroid-refractory; T-cell depletion product improvement; monitor for skin, liver, GI symptoms weekly through day 100
outcome: 100% resolution with treatment; no GvHD-related deaths

text_summary: Cytokine profiling for CRS prediction and early intervention: Prospective study of 428 patients with serial cytokine measurements (6h, 12h, 24h, 48h, 72h post-infusion). Early predictors of grade ≥3 CRS: 24h IL-6 >100 pg/mL (sensitivity 89%, specificity 72%); 24h IFN-gamma >500 pg/mL (sensitivity 82%, specificity 78%); 12h MCP-1 >1000 pg/mL (sensitivity 76%, specificity 81%). Combined 24h cytokine score (IL-6 + IFN-gamma + ferritin): AUC 0.91 for grade ≥3 CRS prediction. Early intervention (tocilizumab at 24h based on cytokine trigger) reduced grade ≥3 CRS from 18% to 5% (p<0.001) without impacting CAR-T efficacy (ORR 73% vs 71%, p=NS). Proposed algorithm: measure IL-6 + IFN-gamma at 24h; if combined score exceeds threshold, administer preemptive tocilizumab.
incidence_rate: Grade ≥3 CRS: reduced from 18% to 5% with early intervention
management_protocol: Measure IL-6 + IFN-gamma + ferritin at 24h post-infusion; combined cytokine score; preemptive tocilizumab if score exceeds threshold; does not impair CAR-T efficacy; reduces ICU admissions by 60%
outcome: Grade ≥3 CRS reduction from 18% to 5%; no efficacy impact

text_summary: Solid tumor TME immunosuppression after CAR-T: Comprehensive analysis of tumor biopsies from 89 solid tumor CAR-T trial patients (mesothelin, GPC3, HER2, EGFR). CAR-T cells detected in tumors of 72% of patients, but 85% of infiltrating CAR-T showed exhaustion phenotype (PD-1+ TIM-3+ LAG-3+ TOX+) by day 14. TME factors: TGF-beta elevation (3.2x vs pre-treatment) in 78%, MDSC frequency increase in 65%, regulatory T-cell (Treg) increase in 58%, PD-L1 upregulation in 71%. Strategies to overcome: dnTGFbRII armoring (4x improved infiltration), checkpoint receptor knockout (enhanced persistence), combination with anti-PD-1 (ORR improvement from 18% to 35%), oncolytic virus pre-treatment (improved trafficking). Conclusion: solid tumor CAR-T requires combination approaches to overcome the TME.
incidence_rate: T-cell exhaustion in 85% of tumor-infiltrating CAR-T
management_protocol: Armored CAR-T designs (dnTGFbRII, IL-12, IL-21); checkpoint knockout (PD-1, LAG-3); combination with anti-PD-1/PD-L1; oncolytic virus pre-treatment; regional delivery to bypass trafficking barriers
outcome: Single-agent CAR-T insufficient for most solid tumors; combination approaches needed
reporting_source: Translational analysis across solid tumor CAR-T trials

text_summary: ICU management protocol for grade 4 CRS: Standardized ICU protocol from 15 high-volume CAR-T centers. Grade 4 CRS definition: life-threatening hypotension, respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation, or multi-organ dysfunction. Incidence: 2-5% across products. Management: immediate ICU transfer; tocilizumab 8mg/kg (can repeat x3 q8h); methylprednisolone 2mg/kg q12h; vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line, vasopressin if refractory); mechanical ventilation (lung-protective strategy); consider anakinra 200mg IV q8h if HLH features; ruxolitinib 10mg BID if refractory; consider siltuximab 11mg/kg if tocilizumab failed (directly neutralizes IL-6 vs blocking receptor). Emergency interventions: plasmapheresis for refractory cases; cyclophosphamide 1g/m2 as ultimate CAR-T elimination. Median ICU stay 3 days (range 1-21). Mortality with protocol: 3% (vs 12% historical without standardized approach).
incidence_rate: 2-5% of all CAR-T recipients
management_protocol: ICU protocol: tocilizumab 8mg/kg q8h (max 3 doses); methylprednisolone 2mg/kg q12h; vasopressors; ventilator support; anakinra 200mg IV q8h if HLH; ruxolitinib 10mg BID; siltuximab if tocilizumab failed; plasmapheresis for refractory; cyclophosphamide as last resort
